title: Mixed-fibre pulp blending, combining cotton and abaca
subtitle: How blending short and long fibres changes sheet character
excerpt: Cotton linter and abaca fibre produce sheets with very different properties. Cotton is short-fibred, opaque, and strong in tension; abaca is long-fibred, translucent, and strong in tear. Blending the two at different ratios produces a range of sheet characters suited to different applications.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923): Hunter documents the use of mixed-fibre stocks in Asian papermaking traditions and in European early modern production. The abaca fibre (Manila hemp) is noted by Hunter as producing distinctively strong, thin sheets used in the printing of high-value documents and Bibles.

glossaryTerms.definition: The average length of individual cellulose fibres in the pulp. Long fibres (abaca: 4-6 mm) produce stronger, more translucent sheets; short fibres (cotton linter: 1-1.5 mm) produce denser, more opaque sheets.
The mechanical process of refining pulp fibres by crushing and fibrillating them. A blender performs a rough equivalent; a Hollander beater produces more refined results.

content.text: Paper character is fibre character. Cotton linter's short fibres form a tight, dense sheet that feels solid and opaque, excellent for writing and printing surfaces. Abaca's long fibres form an open, translucent sheet that tears cleanly but holds less ink without feathering. A 70:30 cotton-to-abaca blend produces a sheet with the density of cotton and some of abaca's tear resistance, a useful all-round bookbinding endpaper.

items.name: Cotton linter (half-stuff sheets)
items.notes: The dominant fibre in most blends. Soak in water for 30 minutes before blending.

items.name: Abaca fibre (prepared)
items.notes: Soak separately from the cotton for 60 minutes, abaca requires a longer soak. Blend separately and combine in the vat.

items.name: Paper mould + deckle, A5
items.notes: For test pulls at each blend ratio.

items.name: Formation aid (PEO)
items.notes: Optional but recommended for abaca-heavy blends, abaca's long fibres clump without it.

content.text: Blend the cotton linter and abaca separately with water to a smooth slurry in the blender, each for thirty to forty-five seconds at high speed. Do not blend them together in the blender; combine them in the vat. The different
intensity, but mixing in the blender tends to produce an uneven distribution.

Pull test sheets at three ratios: 100% cotton, 70% cotton / 30% abaca, and 50/50. Dry all three and hold each to a window. The progression from opaque to translucent should be visible. 100% cotton is dense and matte; 100% abaca is tissue-like and almost transparent; 70/30 is the functional sweet spot for most bookbinding applications.

Abaca fibres are 4-6 times longer than cotton fibres. In the vat they tangle and form mats, which produces a sheet with uneven formation, a streaky or clumpy texture visible when backlit. Adding formation aid (polyethylene oxide, PEO) at 0.2-0.3% by volume of the vat water makes the liquid more viscous and slippery, which delays fibre settling and produces significantly more even distribution across the mould.
